### Node B: Distributed / Competitive Failure

The second crowded branch. Catastrophe here needs no single system to win; it comes from many systems and actors interacting, with no one in control. Two worldviews share the multipolar branch and disagree on what drives the failure: misaligned actors racing, or misaligned subsystems accreting.

The first is racing. Everyone at the frontier says they would prefer a slower, more careful world, but no lab can enforce a slowdown alone, so sitting out just hands control to a competitor they trust less than themselves. Anthropic calls its own existence a "calculated bet" on exactly this logic, and DeepMind reasons the same way. The danger is not a rogue system seizing power; it is unsupervised competition. State militaries, corporate labs, and open-source advocates are locked in a game where coordination is nearly impossible and racing is rewarded, and catastrophe arrives through bargaining failures, the erosion of human control, and models shipped half-vetted because a rival shipped first. Andrew Critch, Stuart Russell, and Toby Ord hold versions of this, with Helen Toner nearby. Critch puts extinction near 85% over about 25 years, roughly 35% from loss-of-control events and 50% from "industrial dehumanization," humans losing economic and political relevance in a machine economy no one steers, and explicitly not from a sovereign AGI. Russell treats safety as an arms-race problem and wants nuclear-style nonproliferation for compute, an IAEA model. The load-bearing facts here are institutional and game-theoretic, not features of any one model: a perfectly aligned AI still kills you if the firm that builds it is out-competed and absorbed by one fielding an unaligned one. What would falsify it is durable international governance that changes the incentives, not voluntary commitments but mechanisms that make defecting on safety costly.

The second is structural disempowerment. Picture markets run by high-frequency algorithms, logistics routed by autonomous systems, power balanced by grid AI. No node decides to remove humanity. But the cost of swapping these systems back for slower human oversight climbs until intervention is priced out, and control becomes administrative, a signature on decisions made elsewhere. This is Gradual Disempowerment: capable systems with goals slightly off from human welfare accrete power until we are nominally in charge and actually not. The argument runs on orthogonality and instrumental convergence, that intelligence and goals are independent, and that almost any goal breeds subgoals to survive and gather resources. Joe Carlsmith and John Wentworth develop it. Unlike racing, the driver is not human competition but the slow handoff of infrastructure to misaligned optimizers, and the reasoning is prior-first: orthogonality and convergence are claims about optimization, not extrapolations from how today's models write code. The fix is upstream, formal alignment guarantees before integration, not after the alternative has been priced out. What would falsify it is a scalable alignment method with real guarantees, or evidence that instrumental convergence is weak at the capability levels that matter.

::: {.aside}
Same branch, different engine. Both views reject the single-optimizer story and sit on the multipolar branch, but racing is driven by human actors in a coordination trap and updates on institutional facts, while disempowerment is driven by misaligned subsystems accreting power and reasons from structural priors. They look adjacent and share almost no standard of evidence, which is the kind of hidden non-overlap this map exists to surface.
:::
